ace_test_recorded_messages.overview: Everything about recorded .mxml message files in ACE v13 test projects: how to enable recording, the file naming convention, checkpoint structure, how to map checkpoints to node-level tests, common failure patterns caused by recorded data, and how to read/parse .mxml files manually. For Java test code that consumes these files, see 5b_java_test_patterns.xml. For the step-by-step workflow, see 5_ace_test_project_guide.xml.

recorded_messages.description: The ACE RecordedMessageManager captures message assemblies at every node transition in a running flow. Each captured checkpoint is saved as an .mxml file. These files are the PREFERRED source of test data because they contain real runtime message trees.

how_to_enable_recording.description: Enable recording in the integration server overrides/server.conf.yaml

file_naming_convention.description:
Recorded files follow the naming convention: [messageId]-[propagationCount].mxml
The propagationCount is a sequential integer starting at 0 for the input node. Each subsequent node transition increments it by 1.
file_naming_convention.example: 000071A0-6A4EAD36-00000001-0.mxml ← propagationCount=0: HTTP Input → out (the trigger) 000071A0-6A4EAD36-00000001-1.mxml ← propagationCount=1: Compute → out 000071A0-6A4EAD36-00000001-2.mxml ← propagationCount=2: HTTP Request → out (backend response)

checkpoint_structure.description:
Each .mxml file is a MessageAssembly snapshot. It encodes four internal trees: - message: headers and payload at this point - localEnvironment: LocalEnvironment (HTTP, WrittenDestination, etc.) - environment: Environment variables set by nodes - exceptionList: any exceptions at this point
A checkpoint element captures WHERE in the flow this snapshot was taken:

checkpoint_fields.field: Unique identifier grouping all checkpoints for one message invocation
Sequential position in the flow (0 = first node output)
Depth of the node call stack when captured
Exact time the checkpoint was recorded

The node that EMITTED the message: - name: node label (matches the label used in SpyObjectReference.node("...")) - type: node type (ComIbmComputeNode, ComIbmWSInputNode, etc.) - terminal: which terminal fired (out, failure, out1, etc.) - application, messageFlow, subflow path - inputNode: TRUE if this is an input-type node
The node that will RECEIVE the message (same fields as source)

checkpoint_structure.how_to_read_checkpoint: - The SOURCE identifies WHICH NODE produced this message and via WHICH TERMINAL - The TARGET identifies which node will receive it - For node N under test: use the checkpoint where TARGET=N as INPUT, and the checkpoint where SOURCE=N as the expected OUTPUT

mapping_to_tests.description:
Map recorded checkpoints to node-level tests as follows: For each node N in the flow: - INPUT to node N = the .mxml where TARGET.node = N (i.e., propagationCount = N's position - 1) - OUTPUT of node N = the .mxml where SOURCE.node = N (i.e., propagationCount = N's position)
Example for a flow: HTTP Input(0) → Prepare Request(1) → HTTP Request(2) → HTTP Reply Node "Prepare Request": input = checkpoint -0 (SOURCE=HTTP Input, TARGET=Prepare Request) output = checkpoint -1 (SOURCE=Prepare Request, TARGET=HTTP Request) Node "HTTP Request": input = checkpoint -1 (SOURCE=Prepare Request, TARGET=HTTP Request) output = checkpoint -2 (SOURCE=HTTP Request, TARGET=HTTP Reply)

recorded_messages.where_to_place_mxml_files: Copy the .mxml files from the RecordedMessageManager output directory into: [AppName]_GeneratedTests/src/main/resources/ They are loaded at runtime via the classloader as classpath resources.

failure.name: timestamp_mismatch
failure.description: The most common JUnit test failure in auto-generated tests. Recorded messages capture timestamps at recording time; when tests re-execute, current system time differs, causing field comparison failures.
failure.example_error: java.lang.AssertionError: Expected: TestMessageAssemblies should have matching message trees. but: Expected value '2025-10-01 15:03:20.104670' but was '2025-10-28 13:49:27.878221' (path: /Message/XMLNSC/db_record_data/createdDate)
failure.fix: Use ignorePath() to exclude the time-varying fields from comparison. Navigate to the line referenced in the error (e.g., tests.java:549) and add ignorePath() calls for the affected paths.

failure.name: propagation_count_mismatch
failure.description: Auto-generated tests assume all node propagations happen simultaneously and independently. In real flows with sequential propagation patterns (Node A → Node B waits for Node C to finish before Node A → Node D), the generated test may expect a propagation that has not occurred yet.
failure.example_pattern: Node A propagates out → Node B → Node C (finishes) → returns to Node A → Node A propagates out1 → Node D. The generated test may expect BOTH Node B and Node D to be called simultaneously, failing with propagation count = 0 on Node D.
failure.example_error: java.lang.AssertionError: Expected: Terminal:"out" to have propagate count:1 but: Node "Store Input Message" Terminal:"out" has propagate count:0
failure.fix_option_1: Modify the generated test to remove or adjust the propagation count assertion for the terminal that fires after the sequential execution completes.
failure.recommendation: Propagation count mismatches usually indicate that auto-generated tests are not the right approach for that node. Write a whole-flow integration test instead that tests the complete message propagation lifecycle.

failure.name: ssl_handshake_exception
failure.description: Tests run at the Integration Server level (NOT the Integration Node level). SSL/TLS configurations defined in node.conf.yaml (truststore, keystore, certificates) are NOT applied during test execution. This causes SSLHandshakeException when tests attempt HTTPS connections.
failure.example_error: TestException(BIP3165) BIP3165E: An error occurred whilst performing an SSL socket operation. Operation: connect. Error Text: javax.net.ssl.SSLHandshakeException: No X509TrustManager implementation available.
failure.fix:
Copy the required security configuration entries from node.conf.yaml into the execution group's overrides/server.conf.yaml:
# In servers/<execution_group_name>/overrides/server.conf.yaml BrokerRegistry: brokerTruststoreFile: '/path/to/truststore.jks'
failure.rule: Always place security configuration in server.conf.yaml, not node.conf.yaml, when it is needed for test execution.

failure.name: generated_tests_brittle
failure.description: Auto-generated tests are tightly coupled to the recorded messages and are brittle — any change to the application may require regenerating and reimporting tests.

symptoms.symptom: Tests that passed yesterday fail after a minor code change
Large number of ignorePath() calls needed to maintain tests
Tests difficult to understand without reading both .mxml files and generated Java

failure.recommendation: Use generated tests as a starting point and for regression testing during migration. For long-lived test suites, invest in manually written whole-flow integration tests that are more resilient to application changes.

bash_script_for_source_target.description: With many .mxml files, manually reading source/target is tedious. Use this bash script with xmllint to quickly parse all files:
bash_script_for_source_target.code:
#!/bin/bash # parse_mxml.sh — print source and target node for each .mxml file # Requires: xmllint (install: sudo snap install libxml2)
for f in *.mxml; do echo "File: $f" src=$(xmllint --xpath \ "string(//*[local-name()='checkpoint']/*[local-name()='source']/*[local-name()='name'])" \ "$f" 2>/dev/null) tgt=$(xmllint --xpath \ "string(//*[local-name()='checkpoint']/*[local-name()='target']/*[local-name()='name'])" \ "$f" 2>/dev/null) echo " Source: $src" echo " Target: $tgt" echo done
bash_script_for_source_target.installation: Linux: sudo apt-get install libxml2-utils Snap: sudo snap install libxml2 Windows: use xmllint from the libxml2 Windows binary package

mapping_table_example.description: Example output from parse_mxml.sh for getCurrentWeather flow:
mapping_table_example.table:
File: 000071A0-6A4EAD36-00000001-0.mxml Source: HTTP Input Target: Prepare Request
File: 000071A0-6A4EAD36-00000001-1.mxml Source: Prepare Request Target: HTTP Request
File: 000071A0-6A4EAD36-00000001-2.mxml Source: HTTP Request Target: HTTP Reply
Therefore: Test "Prepare Request": input=*-0.mxml expected=*-1.mxml terminal=out Test "HTTP Request": input=*-1.mxml expected=*-2.mxml terminal=out (use ignorePath for live response)
